groups.context_description: Term names the kindling of inner anger as the first act of moral rebellion — Cain's anger at divine rejection as the origin-point of the anger characteristic in Scripture

anchors.verse_text: Gen 4:5 but for Cain and his offering he had no regard . So Cain was very angry , and his face fell .

groups.context_description: Term names divine anger as the inner response to Israel's repeated covenant violation — the burning of God's wrath in the moment of particular transgression

anchors.verse_text: Exo 32:10 Now therefore let me alone , that my wrath may burn hot against them and I may consume them, in order that I may make a great nation of you.”

groups.context_description: Term names the inner injunction against anger as spiritual counsel — do not let anger kindle; cease from it; it leads only to harm

anchors.verse_text: Psa 37:8 Refrain from anger , and forsake wrath ! Fret not yourself; it tends only to evil .

meaning_senses.text: to be hot, furious, burn, becomeangry, be kindled
(Niphal) to be angry with, be incensed

context_descriptions: Term names divine indignation as sustained moral disposition — the ongoing inner displeasure of God toward wickedness, felt daily
Term names human indignation as responsive inner emotion — the emotional-moral reaction of moral displeasure arising from provocation or perceived injustice
Term names inner fury driving political and power conflicts — fierce anger in an authority figure expressed through hostile action
Term names inner fury triggered by perceived betrayal — the rage that erupts when deception is discovered, driving violent disproportionate response
Term names divine wrath as the revealed inner response to human unrighteousness and ungodliness — anger as the consistent inner orientation of God toward sin
Term names human anger as requiring governance — the inner state that must be put away, not nursed, as incompatible with righteous living
Term names righteous anger co-existing with grief — the simultaneous presence of anger and sorrow as the inner response to hardness of heart
Term names morally accountable anger — the inner state of anger that exists under divine command and requires governance before it becomes sin
Term names anger as an inner action-driver — the fury that compels the person to respond, act, or enforce consequences
Term names anger as cosmic inner rebellion against God — the hostility of nations and powers in ultimate opposition to divine rule
Term names quick-temperedness as a disqualifying character trait — habitual anger as evidence of inner instability incompatible with leadership
Term names righteous inner spirit-provocation — the spirit of the person stirred to righteous indignation by idolatry and the honour of God
Term names love's non-irritability — the inner disposition of love that displaces reactive anger and refuses to be provoked
Term names the sharp inner conflict of disagreement — the cutting edge of contention between persons of conviction
Term names the deliberate inner-stimulation toward love and good works — the intentional provoking of others' inner life toward what is good
Term names anger as a provoked inner response to injustice or disobedience — the arousal of wrath in another as consequence of conduct
Term names the anger that hardens into danger without resolution — the sunset-bounded inner state that must be addressed before becoming settled resentment
Term names divine relational anger — God's inner anger toward specific persons in response to covenant violation, expressed personally and resolved personally
Term names divine anger as the response to idolatry — the kindling of God's wrath at Israel's most direct covenant violation
Term names divine anger as tempered by steadfast love — the characterisation of God whose anger is slow and bounded by covenant faithfulness
Term names human anger as the inner response to relational conflict — the kindling of personal wrath in the face of provocation, rejection, or perceived injustice
Term names the mastery of anger as the measure of inner greatness — governing anger as exceeding military conquest in demonstration of inner strength
Term names divine anger as the experience of hiddenness — the face turned away as the form of wrath, producing inner desolation
Term names the inner heat of anger as a smoldering thermal process — the self-sustaining build of inner fury toward eruption, using the oven-and-fire metaphor
Term names divine indignation as purposive instrument of judgment — God's inner wrath deployed purposively against wickedness and idolatry
Term names the prophet's inner state as divinely imparted indignation — God's own indignation placed within the person called to speak for him
Term names rage as inner fury producing visible physical transformation and extreme action — the Aramaic term for anger at its most intense somatic expression
Term names divine wrath as consuming inner fire — the heat of God's anger poured out in judgment, spending itself completely
Term names anger as the inner state that transforms countenance and drives extreme action — human fury at its most visible and disruptive
Term names the inner heat of habitual anger — the fury characteristic as a settled disposition that requires management and produces destruction when uncontrolled
Term names the inner warming process of developing intensity — anger or passionate feeling as a thermal process building from within through meditation
Term names the burning intensity of divine wrath at its maximum — fierce anger as the pinnacle of God's response to covenant violation, carrying cosmic consequences
Term names human burning anger as the inner response to perceived injustice or affront — the fierce heat of personal wrath that drives consequential action
Term names fierce inner anger co-existing with grief and loyalty — the intensity qualifier that characterises anger at its most burning while accompanying care
Term names the provoking of divine vexation — Israel's conduct as the trigger of God's inner anger, creating a recurring covenantal pattern of provocation and response
Term names the inner vexation of relational cruelty — the distress produced in a person's inner life by another's deliberately provocative conduct
Term names the inner lodging of vexation as a marker of folly — anger that settles and remains as a characteristic of the unwise person
Term names the vexation of inner grief and provocation — the broad register of inner distress from existential burden, rivalry, and sorrow expressed before God in prayer
Term names the anger that lodges in the fool's heart — inner vexation as the resident condition of one who lacks inner governance
Term names the conduct that provokes divine inner anger — Israel's actions causing God's vexation, with covenantal consequences
Term names contempt as the inner disposition of treating what is sacred as beneath regard — the spurning of God, his word, and his servants
Term names contempt directed at reproof — the inner rejection of correction that closes the person to growth and repentance
Term names the inner speech of contempt — what the wicked person says in his heart as the location of his dismissal of God
Term names disgrace as an inner condition in extremity — the shame-experience accompanying crisis that overwhelms all capacity for response
Term names blasphemous contempt as the inner disposition of rebellion — the speech-act of reviling that reveals the heart's rejection of what is holy
Term names the inner bearing of grudge — sustained anger retained in the heart against another, named as the interior counterpart to the outer act of vengeance
Term names the duration of divine anger — how long God holds wrath and whether it resolves into mercy, with the answer consistently favouring mercy
Term names the fall of the face as the first bodily expression of inner anger — countenance as the mirror of the inner emotional state
Term names verbal aggression as the outer expression of inner hostility — railing and abuse as manifestations of the contemptuous inner character
Term names divine anger as the inner response to persistent covenant violation — God's wrath kindled by Israel's rebellion against his ways
Term names human anger as the inner response to subordinate failure or betrayal — the superior's wrath provoked by those under his authority
Term names divine anger as momentary against the backdrop of everlasting love — the transient quality of God's wrath contrasted with the permanence of his compassion
Term names the communal consequences of anger — how one person's anger destabilises the whole community around them
Term names the trembling restraint of anger — being agitated without sin, holding inner fury still before God in the privacy of the heart
Term names the somatic terror produced by divine power — the body overwhelmed by the experience of God's glory and judgment
Term names grief-disturbance — the agitation of inner pain expressing itself as trembling and mourning in response to devastating loss
Term names the provocation of divine agitation — conduct that disturbs God's inner relational rest, stirring him to respond
Term names the act of provoking divine inner anger — the causal triggering of God's wrath through covenant violation, with historical consequence
Term names the trembling heart as divinely imposed inner instability — inner anxiety and restlessness given by God as a consequence of covenantal displacement
Term names the prophetically embodied inner state of trembling anxiety — the prophet enacting the inner condition of those under judgment as a sign
Term names the excellent spirit as the seat of extraordinary inner character — wisdom, understanding, and moral quality concentrated in the inner spirit as divine endowment
Term names the hardened spirit as the inner seat of pride — the spirit that stiffens against God, producing arrogant conduct and consequent judgment
Term names the anxious spirit as the inner reception of divine vision — the spirit overwhelmed and troubled by apocalyptic revelation
Term names the inner capacity for endurance under increasing pressure — the ability of the inner person to sustain more difficult circumstances without collapsing
Term names competition as an exposure of inner character — striving for display reveals the poverty of the inner life compared with the practice of justice

meaning_senses.text: to be hot, furious, burn, becomeangry, be kindled
(Niphal) to be angry with, be incensed

context_descriptions: Term names the affliction overcome in Christ — anguish as the condition the world inflicts but Christ has conquered, giving inner peace amid tribulation
Term names affliction as the ground of inner character formation — anguish producing endurance, character, and hope through divine working
Term names the anguish of shared suffering and consolation — the inner participation in Christ's afflictions as the ground of mutual comfort
Term names the anguish of the great tribulation — the maximum suffering of the end times as the context of final purification and divine salvation
Term names the anguish of post-mortem torment — intense inner suffering in the afterlife state, in full consciousness of memory and relationship
Term names the anguish of deep relational distress — inner suffering produced by anticipated permanent separation from those deeply loved
Term names unceasing anguish of heart over the spiritual condition of others — sustained inner suffering grounded in love and grief
Term names inner pangs produced by disordered desire — the self-inflicted suffering of those who love money, piercing themselves with their own craving
Term names inner anguish under eschatological judgment — intense inner-physical suffering that drives hardening rather than repentance
Term names the eschatological abolition of inner pain — the promised end of all anguish in the renewed creation
Term names the anguish of heart that accompanies deep pastoral care — inner compression of spirit expressed in tears and written in love
Term names the collective anguish of peoples at cosmic upheaval — inner perplexity and distress as the familiar order dissolves
Term names the odium-quality of inner moral failure — the stench-metaphor expressing how wickedness and folly produce corruption visible to others
Term names the burning intensity of divine wrath at its maximum — fierce anger as the pinnacle of God's response to covenant violation, carrying cosmic consequences
Term names human burning anger as the inner response to perceived injustice or affront — the fierce heat of personal wrath that drives consequential action
Term names fierce inner anger co-existing with grief and loyalty — the intensity qualifier that characterises anger at its most burning while accompanying care
Term names purposive formation — the inner act of planning or forming a purpose, whether divine sovereign intention or human scheme
Term names the aching pain of the inner being — the heart that aches beneath laughter, the affliction brought to God, the pain God inflicts and heals
Term names the constraining quality of anguish and its resolution — inner experience of being hemmed in, cramped, and the divine deliverance to spaciousness
Term names the inner-being pain and sorrow of the person — the anguish that God sees and hears, that fills the days under the sun, and that the Servant of the Lord bears vicariously
Bitterness as inner state caused by grief — specific pain of a parent whose child has brought shame; inner sorrow carrying quality of bitterness
State of being filled with bitterness — inner person pervaded by bitter anguish, here in context of divine affliction that allows no relief
Term names the extreme distress of covenantal curse — the crushing inner and circumstantial compression of siege and covenant abandonment
Term names personal inner distress met with delight in God's commandments — anguish that does not extinguish inner faith
Term names distress as the condition from which people cry to God — inner compression that produces prayer and from which God delivers
Term names the anguish of eschatological judgment — the inner terror of the day of distress
Term names the narrow straits of extreme inner distress — the pressing constraint from which prayer rises and God responds with spaciousness
Term names the bitterness of soul in deep distress — the inner taste-quality of grief, anguish, and sorrow experienced inwardly
Term names the bitter intensity of anger — the fierce, biting quality of wrath in action and in character
Term names the bitterness of the human condition — the taste of life as toil, futility, and moral poison under the sun
Term names the bitterness of inner experience caused by another — the grief inflicted on the inner being through relational or domestic conflict
Private interior quality of bitterness — heart's own bitter knowledge of itself, known to the inner person alone and incommunicable to any other
Term names the inner-being experience of bitterness — the grief that makes life bitter, provoked by loss and divine action, and that the person names and brings before God
Term names bitter fury as an inner-being state — the rage-bitterness that drives violent action, the inner temperature of a person consumed by enraged opposition
Inner condition of being saturated with bitterness — inner person overwhelmed with bitter suffering inflicted by divine judgment
Term names the anguish of concern for another in spiritual peril — the inner distress of love that cannot rest when those it cares for are at risk
Term names the inner-being sorrow and injury that afflicts the person — sorrows that multiply for those who follow other gods, the crushed spirit, and the wounds that God heals
Term names the communal cry of joy and praise — the expressive inner jubilation voiced by peoples and places in response to divine acts
Term names the narrow distress from which the person cries to God and receives divine response with spaciousness
Term names distress as the divinely appointed condition that produces return to God — anguish as the instrument of covenantal restoration
Term names the anguish of being given over to enemies — distress as the covenantal consequence of rebellion, with deliverance as its resolution
Term names anguish as a reliable divine refuge — the God who is found present precisely in times of distress
Term names the distress of the righteous as the condition from which God delivers — anguish as the ground of divine rescue
Term names the anguish of crisis as the place of prayer and divine hearing — distress driving the person to call upon God with the expectation of response
Term names evil as the circumstantial expression of anguish — the distress and calamity that befalls the person as the condition from which God hides and delivers
Term names the inner recognition of one's own evil conduct — the naming of wrongdoing by the person who has done it as a moral self-assessment
Term names the inner complaint or meditation poured out before God — the sustained outpouring of anguish, petition, and reflective address that constitutes the deepest inner communication with God
Term names meditation as the sustained inner engagement with God's law and presence — the all-day practice of the devoted heart that produces understanding
Term names the inner capacity for endurance under increasing pressure — the ability of the inner person to sustain more difficult circumstances without collapsing
Term names competition as an exposure of inner character — striving for display reveals the poverty of the inner life compared with the practice of justice

Term names the physical act of embrace as the expression of an inner state of fulfilment and devotion — the arms receiving what the inner person has waited for; the body as the outward form of inward reception
Term names the acute inner distress of the person facing suffering and death — the troubled, anguished state of the inner being at the limit of what it can bear
Term names the inner distress arising from relational concern — the person troubled in themselves because of the impact of their condition on those they love

Term names compulsion on inner religious identity and practice — external pressure forcing conformity in the domain of faith, circumcision, and covenantal belonging; the inner-being orientation of the person as the object of constraint

Term names compulsion that overrides or presses on the inner will and disposition — the person constrained to act against or beyond their own inclination; inner orientation overridden by external force
Voluntary vs compelled inner orientation in service
Existential distress and hardship as experienced inner pressure
Compulsion vs willing inner orientation — freedom of will
Overwhelming inner alarm and awe-struck response
Utter astonishment as inner affective response
Amazement as inner affective response to unexpected authority or teaching
Affliction and being pressed as inner-bearing experience of suffering
Distress and oppression as inner and social suffering
Grief and sorrow as spontaneous inner affective response
Grief caused by another — interpersonal pain
Godly grief producing inner transformation and repentance
Being troubled/harassed as inner state of distress and helplessness
Constrained/pressed in inner being — restriction of soul and affections
Distress as existential suffering condition
Inner compulsion, constraint, and distress as motivating or anguishing force
Being seized with fear as inner affective state
Iniquity and wickedness as inner moral quality — conceived, spoken, enacted
Trouble and toil as an experienced inner condition of life
Distress and displeasure as inner affective state
Urgent haste as expression of inner urgency or determination
Dismay and terror as overwhelming inner affective state
Rashness and hasty inner disposition
Alarm, anxiety and troubled thoughts as inner affective state
Urgent haste as expression of inner alarm or urgency
Cry of distress and plea to God as expression of inner anguish or urgency
Cry of grief, anguish or mourning as outward expression of inner pain
Cry of outcry against injustice, reproach or moral outrage
Outcry as expression of suffering, affliction and plea for help
Outcry as communal lamentation — grief and mourning expressed publicly
Distress and anguish as experienced inner condition — fear, torment, suffering
God's forming of the inner person — heart and spirit
Potter-clay metaphor as inner-being orientation — human dependence, formation, and moral shaping before God
The adversary relationship as context of inner appeal and conflict
Pain as experienced inner suffering — unassuaged, incurable, of the heart
Anxiety of spirit as inner affective state
Digging/plotting as expression of inner malice and scheming
Opened ear as inner receptivity to God
Crushing burden as inner-bearing experience of oppression
Siege as context engaging inner trust and watchful orientation
Oppression as force bearing down on the inner person — the experience of being driven, pressed, afflicted
Grief and inner pain — being grieved, distressed, or pained at heart
Idol as object of inner attachment, devotion and spiritual bondage
Oppressive pressure as experienced inner-bearing condition
Disaster as context engaging inner moral response — contempt, rejoicing at another's ruin, or desperate cry
Outcry as expression of inner pain, anguish and distress — from heart to voice
Inner constraint and compulsion — the spirit pressed from within
Oppressive pressing from without — distress experienced under siege and oppression
Distress as experienced condition of troubled time
Anguish as experienced inner condition of distress and darkness
Being hemmed in, distressed and confined — the experience of inner constraint before God or suffering
Provocation and harassment as hostile inner-driven action — stirring up enmity and opposition
Rock as divine epithet — God as the object of inner trust, refuge and security
Rock forgotten or abandoned — inner apostasy and unfaithfulness to God
Narrowness and constraint as inner-bearing experience of distress and limitation
Hardness of constraint as inner-bearing experience — adversity and envious limitation
Times of distress as context of inner appeal and trust in God
The vexer as source of inner distress — provocation grieving the spirit
Being in distress, constrained and afflicted — inner experience of pressure and suffering
Iniquity bound up — stored guilt as inner moral condition
Distress as acute inner condition — the moment of extremity, terror, and desperate appeal to God
The adversary/foe as source of inner distress — enmity, reproach, and the experience of being vexed
Rivalry as relational dynamic producing inner jealousy and distress
Hardening of heart and stubborn will — inner moral resistance to God
Inner character of harshness and severity — expressed in speech and conduct
Stubbornness and stiff-necked resistance — inner moral obstinacy
Harshness and rough treatment as inner character quality expressed outward
Broken spirit under harsh oppression — inner bearing of suffering
Contending with God — inner existential struggle and appeal for divine response
Pleading one's cause — inner appeal to God or human authority for justice and vindication
Quarrelling and contention as inner conflict — grievance, frustration, and dispute between persons
Strife and contention as inner conflict — quarrelling, dispute, and the experience of ongoing enmity
Pleading one's cause — inner appeal for justice and divine advocacy
Indictment and controversy — God's case against his people as relational-moral accusation
Bad news and evil report as occasion of inner fear, grief or sadness
Bad/harmful spirit as inner torment — the harmful spirit afflicting Saul
Evil experienced as inner-bearing suffering condition — unhappy business, grievous evil
Evil as inner moral disposition — wickedness conceived in the heart, intended, and enacted
Evil as experienced suffering — distress, harm, and calamity borne by persons
Evil repaid and returned — the moral economy of wrong done and consequences borne
Being evil and doing wickedly — inner moral disposition enacted
Displeasure and anger as inner affective response — being displeased, grieved, or made sad
Harm and calamity as inner-bearing experience — being treated harshly, brought low, or suffering hurt
Meditation and inner pondering — reflective contemplation of God's works and word
Complaint and lament as inner expression of distress
Exhausting toil as inner-bearing weariness
